data.title: Top 10 Dividend Stocks For Uncertain Times
data.description:
When Market Volatility Strikes

A global sell-off erased early gains the S&P 500 posted in 2026, driven by U.S. trade tensions with Europe along with geopolitical friction over Greenland. Stress in Japan’s bond market also contributed to the renewed volatility, which comes on top of sticky inflation, mixed labor signals, and concerns over elevated valuations.

In periods like this, a fixed-income source can help relieve investor anxiety by offering a steady stream of cash and downside protection. Dividends and reinvestment have contributed significantly to shareholder returns throughout the ups and downs of economic cycles.

Protecting your portfolio requires more than buying high-yield stocks - safety, growth, and consistency are critical factors in finding the best opportunities. Seeking Alpha’s Dividend Grades have demonstrated accuracy in avoiding cuts and delivering growth. The grading system flags dividends at risk by analyzing crucial indicators such as payout ratio, interest coverage, cash flow, operating leverage, and debt levels.

SA Quant assembled a list of 10 Strong Buy dividend stocks that are well-equipped for potentially navigating market uncertainty. The list showcases collective strength across dividend yield and excellent all-around investment fundamentals like momentum, profitability, and earnings revisions.

How I Chose Top Dividend Stocks

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I filtered for stocks built for uncertain market conditions, including strengths across dividend safety, growth, yield, and consistency. This basket of 10 stocks has an average dividend yield of ~4%. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SPY) yields 1.05%, and the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) 1.58%. Each stock on the list offers both strong dividends and Strong Buy investment fundamentals.

1. Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE)

  • Market Capitalization: $269.76M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Diversified REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 4 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 1 out of 12

  • Dividend Yield: 6.50%

Alpine Income Property Trust is a high-yielding REIT with a solid track record of growth, offering a diverse portfolio of 128 properties across 34 states, boasting a 99% occupancy rate. The portfolio features top tenants such as Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW), DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. (DKS), and Walgreens.

Rent escalations and higher interest income boosted revenue and earnings growth as Alpine stepped up the size and pace of investments. Alpine continued to enhance the credit quality of its portfolio, and by the end of Q3 2025, Lowe's and Dick's Sporting Goods represented 48% of annualized base rent.

Robust AFFO generation over the past five years supports Alpine’s consistent dividend, which offers an attractive yield vs. the real estate sector median. PINE’s dividend has a 5Y growth rate of 6.81% and has been paid out consistently since its 2019 IPO.

PINE is trading at an attractive valuation with a P/AFFO of ~9.6x, a discount to the sector of about 38%. PINE is a great dividend stock that is a well-positioned REIT to stabilize your portfolio amid uncertain times.

2. Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.70B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 13 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 2 out of 14

  • Dividend Yield: 5.21%

HST has ridden the post-pandemic leisure and travel rebound to deliver strong AFFO in the past year and offers a growing and reliable dividend. HST has a portfolio of 80 hotels under major brands concentrated in top markets across the United States.

HST was able to overcome headwinds amid macroeconomic uncertainty in Q3 2025 to deliver solid results. Revenue per Available Room (RevPAR) growth exceeded expectations, driven by short-term leisure transient demand. Looking ahead, HST is optimistic about the leisure transient demand recovery in Maui and expects 10 markets to benefit from the World Cup, especially New York and Washington, DC. And long-term prospects look solid, with FFO projected to grow over the next two years, according to consensus estimates.

Strong growth in AFFO and a solid balance sheet underpin HST’s A+ Dividend Safety Grade, along with a reasonable payout ratio and exceptional interest coverage. HST is also crushing the sector in dividend growth, with a 5Y growth rate of more than 30%.

Despite outperforming the sector in price performance in the past year, HST is trading at an attractive valuation at 12x AFFO. HST’s safe, high-yielding, and growing dividend has the potential to provide investors with steady income.

3. Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY)

  • Market Capitalization: $110.38B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Health Care

  • Industry: Pharmaceuticals

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 36 out of 971

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 8 out of 179

  • Dividend Yield: 4.65%

Bristol-Myers Squibb’s diverse portfolio balances growth and mature therapies across cardiovascular, oncology, immunology, and hematology. The stock posted excellent momentum in the past three months but fell last week after the Trump administration unveiled proposed drug-pricing reforms.

BMY surged after it posted $12.22B in revenue in Q3 2025 (+6% YoY) and improved guidance, driven by strong performance for its two best-selling drugs. Global revenue from Eliquis, an oral blood thinner, rose 25% to $3.75B. BMY’s cancer treatment Opdivo rose 7% to $2.5B. Guggenheim analyst Seamus Fernandez upgraded BMY in December, saying that results for several pipeline candidates could lead to upside in 2026, including phase III results for two stroke prevention assets.

The reliability of BMY’s dividend is highlighted by an eye-popping 36 consecutive years of payments—including 18 straight years of growth. BMY’s robust profitability, cash flow, and return on assets underlie solid dividend safety and growth grades, while an A+ Yield Grade is driven by a forward yield that crushes the health care sector median.

Trading at an attractive 8.5x earnings, BMY’s PEG ratio of 0.13 represents a 92% discount to the sector, supporting an ‘A’ Valuation Grade. As confidence in the market remains fragile amid trade tensions and mixed economic data, BMY’s consistent dividend offers a potential semblance of certainty.

4. Lincoln National Corporation (LNC)

  • Market Capitalization: $7.55B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Life and Health Insurance

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 15 out of 684

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 2 out of 18

  • Dividend Yield: 4.53%

Lincoln National Corporation’s momentum rose sharply in the past year, far outperforming the financial sector while reporting solid earnings results. The Pennsylvania-based life and health insurer derives most of its operating earnings from annuities, group protection, retirement planning, and life insurance and is backed by a high-quality and growing $126B investment portfolio.

LNC 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Financial Sector (XLF)

Revenue rose by 10% YoY to $4.56B in Q3 2025, and adjusted operating profit rose by 13% to $397M, on solid annuities performance resulting from favorable equity markets and higher spread income. Life insurance earnings surged on stable mortality rates, increased investment income, and operational efficiencies.

LNC has returned value to shareholders in the form of dividend payments for 36 consecutive years, supported by outstanding liquidity that undergirds its Dividend Safety Grade. In addition to a strong cash position, the dividend safety is also bolstered by a conservative payout ratio, a solid sustainable growth rate, and a dividend coverage ratio.

A dividend yield well above the sector median reinforces LNC’s discounted valuation, providing tangible cash returns that validate the stock’s low multiples. LNC is trading at a remarkable 5x earnings while its PEG and EV/EBIT ratios represent substantial discounts to the sector.

LNC’s exceptional balance sheet, high-quality investment portfolio, and capital efficiency position the company well for awarding investors with a solid long-term dividend.

5. COPT Defense Properties (CDP)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Office REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 15 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 1 out of 18

  • Dividend Yield: 4.00%

A REIT focused on U.S. defense and contractor facilities, COPT is well-positioned to capitalize on the historic boost in security spending and budget request from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). COPT’s portfolio spans roughly 24.5 million square feet, with 96% under lease, concentrated in mission-critical defense/IT installations.

An increase in average occupancy and lower net operating and interest expenses drove same-property NOI up 4.6% in Q3 2025 and FFO by +6.2% to $0.69. COPT has a strong pipeline of deals in advanced negotiations and raised its target for vacancy leasing by 11% to 500K square feet. Backed by a portfolio expansion riding defense megatrends, COPT expects to see solid long-term growth in funds from operations, CEO Stephen Budorick said in the earnings call:

“Our Defense/IT investment strategy, which concentrates our portfolio near priority U.S. defense installations, continued to generate excellent results during the third quarter,” Budorick said. “We expect a strong fourth quarter and we continue to anticipate compound annual FFO per share growth of over 4% between 2023 to 2026.”

Strong AFFO payout ratios and interest coverage are key factors driving an A- Dividend Safety Grade, while forward growth metrics, backed by consensus dividend targets over the next 3 years, support COPT’s solid growth grade. Through a disciplined capital allocation strategy, COPT has been able to deliver 27 consecutive years of dividend payments.

The defense property REIT comes at a slight premium to the sector, but solid AFFO growth, exceptional return on equity, and a reliable dividend support COPT’s Strong Buy Quant Rating.

6. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.88B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 3 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 1 out of 14

  • Dividend Yield: 3.92%

The #1 quant-ranked Hotel & Resort REIT, DiamondRock Hospitality, owns a portfolio of 36 properties concentrated in resort destinations and urban gateways, with half of its earnings deriving from its top 6 markets: Boston, Chicago, New York City, the Florida Keys, Fort Lauderdale, and Vail. DRH has excellent all-around fundamentals, with A’s across four factor grades and a solid valuation score. Improving momentum and growth grades in the past three months have pushed DRH squarely into Strong Buy territory.

Despite the ongoing political and economic environment, DRH navigated headwinds to outperform industry RevPAR trends in Q3 2025. Reflecting resilient performance despite macro volatility, DRH lifted its full-year adjusted EBITDA and FFO guidance. DRH expects to reach new highs in revenue in 2026 on unique industry tailwinds, including FIFA World Cup-related demand. The event could lead to outsized gains for DRH, according to SA Analyst The Investment Doctor:

“2026 could be a positive surprise for DiamondRock as it has excellent exposure to the World Cup soccer. That traditionally paves the way to hike the room rates… and should have a positive impact on its bottom line result.”

The Q3 results contributed to strong growth in AFFO and cash flow, which have been pivotal to the dividend’s amazing 3-year CAGR of 74% and A+ Dividend Growth Grade. Meanwhile, conservative leverage and reasonable payout ratios drive a top-notch Dividend Safety Grade.

DRH’s price-to-AFFO ratio of 10x marks a roughly 35% discount to the sector, a solid value for a REIT poised to exploit travel industry tailwinds while, at the same time, providing a stable dividend for enduring any potential storms.

7. Phillips 66 (PSX)

  • Market Capitalization: $55.79B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 18 out of 223

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 3 out of 18

  • Dividend Yield: 3.47%

One of my Top Stocks With High EPS Growth Targets, Phillips 66 offers a diverse portfolio across midstream, refining, chemicals, marketing, and renewables, and has soundly outperformed energy sector peers in the last 12 months.

Phillips 66 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Energy Sector (XLE)

Shares rose after PSX reported stronger-than-expected refining margins and operational execution across core assets in Q3 2025. PSX posted 99% refinery utilization—its highest quarterly level since 2018—along with a notable reduction in adjusted controllable costs. The renewable fuels and chemicals segment delivered strong earnings, supported by higher margins tied to the company’s low-cost feedstock advantage.

The company’s Q3 results reinforce a positive outlook, driven by an exceptional long-term forward earnings growth rate, while EPS is expected to soar in FY 2026. Reflecting bullish momentum from Wall Street analysts, PSX has 18 upward earnings revisions in the last 90 days to 2 downward revisions.

Offering a highly consistent dividend, PSX has posted 13 consecutive years of dividend payments and growth, a track record that is projected to continue over the next three years. Driven by an excellent growth forecast, the stock’s PEG represents a discount to the sector of over 40%, although the overall valuation framework is neutral. Improving margins, cash flow, and cost control provide further support to Phillips 66’s dividend, which has an established track record of consistent growth.

8. Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK)

  • Market Capitalization: $271.66B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Health Care

  • Industry: Pharmaceuticals

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 25 out of 971

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 7 out of 179

  • Dividend Yield: 3.11%

Merck’s momentum has accelerated in the past three months, boosted by analyst upgrades, acquisitions, product launches, and a deep pipeline. Wolfe Research Analyst Alexandria Hammond upgraded the stock to outperform, arguing that Merck is well-positioned to handle the loss of exclusivity for its flagship IV cancer drug Keytruda by shifting over 40% of sales to its newly approved subcutaneous version by 2029.

Beating earnings estimates for the sixteenth straight quarter, Merck in Q3 2025 saw revenue grow by 3.7% to $17.3B and net income by over 60% driven by increased Keytruda and animal health segment sales. Merck has 80 Phase III trials underway and is investing in 20 programs that represent a $50B revenue opportunity by the mid-2030s. Merck’s robust operating and cash flow margins drive an A+ Profitability Grade.

Merck’s strong cash flow generation and exceptional growth in core profits have been keys to its stellar Dividend Scorecard. High interest coverage and sustainable growth support its A- Dividend Safety Grade. Strong forward growth in profits and cash flow, in addition to a high degree of operating leverage, bolsters Merck’s excellent Dividend Growth Grade. Merck has delivered 36 consecutive years of dividend payouts, including 15 years of growth.

Despite a strong dividend and earnings growth upside, Merck is trading at a solid valuation with PEG at a discount to the sector of ~30%. A safe, growing, and consistent dividend, supported by strong profitable growth, makes Merck a top dividend stock with the potential to provide steady income in an uncertain market. My next pick is another pharmaceutical giant with a Strong Buy quant rating and a high-quality dividend.

9. Valero Energy Corporation (VLO)

  • Market Capitalization: $56.34B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 9 out of 223

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 2 out of 18

  • Dividend Yield: 2.45%

Valero has crushed the market in total return to shareholders over the past five years and has soundly outperformed in price performance in the past 12 months. Valero was among the energy stocks that soared earlier this month following the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro and the Trump administration’s vow to open up the country for U.S. oil companies.

VLO 5Y Total Return vs. S&P 500 (SPY)

Valero topped earnings expectations in Q3 2025 for a 16th consecutive quarter, with adjusted operating income nearly tripling YoY to $1.7B. Results were driven by exceptional refinery throughput utilization of 97%, with the Gulf Coast and North Atlantic region setting all-time highs. Refining margins remained well supported by strong global demand, low inventory levels, and supply constraints driven by ongoing geopolitical disruptions. Strategically, Valero is advancing a $230M project at its St. Charles refinery designed to increase high-value product yields, with operations expected to begin in H2 2026.

Valero has outstanding earnings growth targets for the next two fiscal years, backed by bullish earnings revisions. Robust cash from operations has enabled Valero to provide a safe dividend, propped up by a solid payout ratio and prudent leverage highlighted by strong net debt to assets and EBITDA. Valero has delivered 28 consecutive years of dividend payments for a B+ Dividend Consistency Grade.

Although VLO is trading at high earnings multiples, the PEG of 0.92 represents a discount to the sector of nearly 40%. With a steady dividend and solid strategic position in the market, VLO is well-positioned to both ride out market volatility and capitalize on potential upside from favorable industry trends.

10. The Hanover Insurance Group, Inc. (THG)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.08B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Property and Casualty Insurance

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 13 out of 684

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/21/2026): 1 out of 53

  • Dividend Yield: 2.22%

Among my top dividend stock picks in November, the Hanover Insurance Group provides coverage for business, personal, and specialty lines across the U.S. A diversified portfolio, robust profitable growth, and pricing power have allowed THG to perform across economic cycles while maintaining margins and offering a potential inflation buffer.

THG posted record quarterly operating earnings per share ($5.09) and operating return on equity (21.1%) in Q3 2025 and saw strong results across each key business segment. According to THG management, the results reflect outstanding execution and disciplined underwriting:

“Underlying performance was strong, driven by the cumulative impact of prior pricing and underwriting actions, which continue to yield excellent results,” CEO John Roche said in a press release. “Market dynamics in our chosen segments remain constructive, with consistent pricing gains in Core Commercial and Specialty thanks to our focus on smaller, less cyclical sectors, while in Personal Lines, our whole-account strategy provides good insulation from the competitive auto market.”

THG has delivered 20 straight years of dividend payments—including 20 consecutive years of growth. Surging operating income and a high degree of operating leverage have driven a solid Dividend Growth Grade. The safety and reliability of the dividend are sustained by balance sheet strength and a disciplined capital allocation framework, highlighted by a payout ratio of ~20% vs. the sector’s 33% and high interest coverage.

THG is trading at an attractive price relative to earnings, cash flow, and operating income for an A- Valuation Grade, while its PEG of 0.08 reflects a 92% discount to the sector. THG wraps up my top 10 dividend stocks for 2026, a basket of picks with solid collective fundamentals, built to endure fluctuations in market conditions.

Top Dividend Stocks For Market Uncertainty

Investor sentiment improved at the start of 2026, only to be rocked by geopolitical tensions and volatility. In this uncertain environment, dividends can offer stability and downside protection through a steady flow of fixed income. YTD, small- and mid-cap stocks, in addition to gold as a safe-haven, have emerged to outperform in 2026 amid market rotation—a benefit of the PRO Quant Portfolio. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps.

High yields alone are insufficient for dividend security—investors need to weigh factors such as safety, growth, and consistency. SA Dividend Grades have a strong record of avoiding cuts and delivering growth by assessing key indicators such as cash flow, payout ratio, and leverage. SA Quant picked ten Strong Buy stocks built to preserve investor capital and endure fluctuating market conditions. The basket of stocks showcases collective strength across dividend yield, safety, growth, and consistency.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. As highlighted above, if you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

data.title: Why Tight Stop-Losses Often Hurt Investors - And What Robust Capital Growth Really Requires
data.description:
By Xinyu Xiong

Ask investors how they manage risk, and many will give the same answer: tight stop-losses. Widely viewed as a cornerstone of disciplined risk management, tight stop-losses can sometimes work against investors’ long-term objectives.

A stop-loss is a predefined rule that forces the exit of an investment position when its price moves against the investor by a specified amount. Its primary purpose is to limit downside losses on an individual position without requiring continuous monitoring. The rationale seems straightforward. By limiting losses on individual positions, investors believe they are exercising discipline and protecting the portfolio from severe drawdowns.

More broadly, the issue touches on three related questions in risk management: the trade-off between precision and robustness, how trade-level rules aggregate into portfolio-level outcomes, and why controls designed for psychological comfort can impair long-term compounding.

In practice, many who rigorously apply tight stop-loss rules experience a frustrating pattern: frequent small losses, occasional gains, and little progress toward durable capital growth. This raises a critical question for long-term investors, portfolio managers, and fiduciaries alike: can widely accepted stop-loss practices be structurally counterproductive? And what can they be replaced with?

When Trade-Level Discipline Conflicts with Portfolio Outcomes

Viewed in isolation, tight stop-losses appear prudent. By defining a small, predetermined loss, investors feel they have transformed uncertainty into something measurable and controllable. Each trade appears safe in isolation, and losses feel disciplined rather than accidental. This provides investors with a level of psychological comfort.

Markets, however, do not reward isolated decisions. They reward sequences of decisions made under uncertainty. In trend-based or breakout strategies (e.g., when an asset or stock moves beyond its target price), profitable opportunities rarely develop smoothly. Early phases are often volatile, marked by reversals and false starts. Narrow stop-losses systematically remove investors during precisely this stage, not because the underlying signal is invalid, but because short-term price fluctuations exceed arbitrarily tight thresholds.

Once stopped out, re-entry is difficult. Recent losses discourage recommitment to the same trade, and prices may have already moved away from the original entry point. The result is a portfolio that avoids large losses but also misses the handful of outsized gains that drive long-term returns.

What looks like good risk control at the trade level can become opportunity destruction at the portfolio level.

The Behavioral Appeal and Cost of Tight Stops

The case against tight stop-losses has become stronger as markets themselves have changed. Modern markets are dominated by algorithmic trading, fragmented liquidity, and automated execution. Prices now move faster, liquidity is more conditional, and short-term volatility is often driven by order flow dynamics rather than information. In this environment, stop-losses behave differently than they did in slower, dealer-driven markets.

The popularity of tight stop-losses reflects their psychological appeal. By defining a small, predetermined loss, investors feel a sense of control. Losses appear disciplined rather than accidental, and regret is minimized, at least in the short term.

But this comfort comes at a cost. Tight stop-losses align closely with behavioral biases such as loss aversion and regret avoidance. They optimize for emotional relief rather than economic outcomes. Markets, however, reward sustained exposure to favorable return distributions, not psychological comfort.

Risk Management is Also About Time in the Market

Discussions about stop-losses often focus narrowly on loss size. But risk is not only about how much is lost when an investment fails, it is also about how long capital remains exposed to opportunity.

Exposure persistence matters because capital growth is multiplicative. Long-term performance depends not only on avoiding losses but on remaining invested long enough to participate in sustained price movements. Truncating exposure too aggressively can be just as damaging as taking excessive losses.

To examine this trade-off more clearly, it helps to move beyond individual trades and decompose performance into three components:

  • Position size
  • Win rate
  • Payoff ratio (average gain relative to average loss)

Stop-loss design directly affects both win rate and payoff ratio — often in opposing directions.

What the Evidence Suggests

To make these trade-offs concrete, it is useful to examine how stop-loss width affects portfolio outcomes when other variables are held constant. Specifically, consider a simple long-only trend-entry framework applied to a broad equity index. Positions are initiated when prices cross above a moving average. Position size is held constant, while stop-loss thresholds vary from very tight to relatively wide levels.

Using daily S&P 500 (SPX) open, high, low, and close prices as a data source, I simulate 500 investors entering at random dates (2000–2005) and compare outcomes under different stop-loss widths and take-profit targets (15%–30%). Each curve summarizes the average result across investors (Figure 1).

The objective is not to identify an optimal trading rule or maximize historical returns. Instead, the goal is to examine how stop-loss width structurally influences win rates, payoff ratios, and cumulative capital growth.

As stop-losses widen, win rates increase. Trades are given more room to absorb short-term noise, reducing premature exits.

Figure 1: Win Rate as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

At the same time, when stop-losses are set farther away from the entry price, the average size of losses increases relative to the average size of gains.

Figure 2: Payoff Ratio as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

When these effects are combined at the portfolio level, cumulative returns plotted against stop-loss width reveal a striking asymmetry: a single peak surrounded by a broad, uneven plateau. Performance deteriorates sharply when stop-losses are too tight but declines only gradually when they are moderately widened beyond the optimal point. This asymmetry is especially evident when higher take-profit targets are considered.

Figure 3: Cumulative Return as a Function of Stop-Loss Width

Why Robustness Matters More Than Precision

The existence of an optimal stop-loss level does not mean it must be identified with precision. Performance is highly fragile on the left side of the return curve, where stop-losses are too tight and small estimation errors, execution frictions, or regime shifts can have outsized negative effects.

On the right side, cumulative returns form a broad plateau. Moderate increases in stop-loss width do not materially impair long-term performance.

This asymmetry suggests a shift in perspective. Robust capital growth is achieved not by operating at the point of maximum expected return but by remaining within a range of parameter resilience.

Accepting slightly wider stop-losses may increase single-trade drawdowns, but it also reduces sensitivity to noise, uncertainty, and behavioral frictions, unavoidable features of real-world investing.

Implications for Long-Term Investors

Tight stop-losses are often perceived as disciplined risk control, but they can unintentionally undermine long-term performance by truncating exposure and amplifying behavioral frictions. In modern markets, robust risk management focuses less on where the stop is placed and more on how exits are structured, timed, and executed.

Rather than asking how tight a stop-loss can be made, investors may benefit from reframing the question:

  • Does this stop-loss allow sufficient time for an opportunity to develop?
  • Am I optimizing for precision or for robustness?
  • Am I minimizing losses or maximizing participation in favorable return distributions?
  • Can I tolerate larger individual losses in exchange for more stable long-term growth?

The Upshot

Risk management is not about eliminating discomfort. It is about choosing which discomforts are worth enduring. By recognizing the structural trade-off between win rate and payoff ratio, and by prioritizing robustness over narrow optimization, investors can design stop-loss frameworks that better align with the realities of market behavior and the mathematics of capital growth.

data.title: Madison Dividend Income Fund Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
For the fourth quarter, the Madison Dividend Income Fund (Class I) returned -0.2%, which compared to the S&P 500 Index, Russell 1000 Value Index and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of +2.7%, +3.8% and +2.9%, respectively.

For the full year, the Dividend Income Fund gained +8.3%, which compared to the S&P 500 Index, Russell 1000 Value Index and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of +17.9%, +15.9% and +15.2%, respectively.

Market performance in the last three years from 2023 to 2025 was dominated by the Magnificent seven stocks, including Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Tesla. Broadcom was another stock with strong returns over this period. In total, these eight stocks accounted for almost 38% of the S&P 500 at year-end. Because the S&P 500 Index is market cap weighted, performance in these names has driven historic outperformance compared to many dividend stocks and other areas of the market, including the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index. Notably, in the past three years, the S&P 500 outperformed the equal weight S&P 500 by 34%, which is the widest 3-year performance gap in history according to Charlie Bilello, the Chief Market Strategist at Creative Planning. As shown in the chart below from Bilello, the prior record was 32% outperformance in 1997-1999, which was followed by a sharp reversal and seven years of equal weight outperformance. Could a similar dynamic occur in this instance over the next few years?

S&P 500 vs. S&P 500 Equal Weight (EQ) (Total Returns, 1971 - 2025)

We believe the answer might be yes. Entering 2026, the S&P 500 is trading near all-time high valuation levels while many dividend stocks and the equal weight S&P 500 are much cheaper. For example, the S&P 500 next 12 months (NTM) price-to-sales ratio expanded from less than 2x in 2022 up to 3.3x at year end, which is historically expensive. By contrast, the NTM price-to-sales ratio for the equal weight S&P 500 was only 1.7x according to FactSet. On this metric, the equal weight S&P 500 is trading at just half the valuation level of the S&P 500. Forward 12-month expected price-to earnings ratio valuations show a similar valuation gap. The forward P/E ratio for the equal-weight S&P 500 is only 17x compared to near 23x for the S&P 500, which is a historic low relative valuation of 0.74x.

Looking at dividend stock valuations, many dividend paying companies are historically cheap compared to the broad market. Our preferred valuation metric is looking at the relative yield of dividend stocks versus the S&P 500. Relative yield is defined as a stock or portfolio dividend yield divided by the S&P 500 dividend yield. A high relative yield compared to its long-term history indicates a low valuation for the stock or portfolio, while a low relative yield ratio indicates a high valuation, all else equal. At year-end, the relative yield of the Dividend Income Fund was 2.25x the S&P 500 and 1.40x the Russell 1000 Value Index, both at the very high end of their historical ranges. In our view, dividend stocks are on sale compared to both indexes.

With S&P 500 valuations at all-time high levels, we believe there are risks of multiple contraction and market correction. The Dividend Income Fund is trying to protect against these risks by owning a diversified portfolio of high-quality, above-average dividend stocks in cheaper areas of the market. We think Dividend Income has important defensive characteristics that help protect capital in down markets. These favorable attributes include:

  • An absolute portfolio dividend yield of 2.53%, which compares favorably to the absolute dividend yields of 1.12% for the S&P 500 and 1.81% for the Russell 1000 Value Index as of year-end.
  • A growing income stream that protects against inflation. Portfolio holdings have increased their dividends by 6% on average over the past year, which is well above inflation rates over that period. Consistent annual dividend increases are a key metric we require in portfolio holdings.
  • A high-quality portfolio with strong balance sheets that could protect on the downside in a market correction. 94% of fund holdings are rated A- or better by Standard & Poor’s, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 at 35% and the Russell 1000 Value at 22%. If there is a down market and/or credit spreads begin to widen, we believe our high-quality portfolio is likely to hold up much better than the overall market.

Summary

The Madison Dividend Income Fund is a conservatively managed equity strategy that owns a high-quality portfolio of above-average dividend yield stocks with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages (wide moats). We believe that owning high-quality stocks with above-average dividend yields is the best way to provide income and generate attractive long-term returns over a full market cycle while limiting drawdowns in bear markets and market corrections.
