data.title: Asymmetric Risks And Shifting Economic Fundamentals: Time To Diversify Beyond AI
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Simmons Partnership as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Written by Ricci L. Reber, Ph.D.

Now that everyone is back from extended holiday breaks and a new trading year has kicked off in earnest, it feels like a good time to reflect on the current state of the economy and markets, examine some factors that I think will drive near-term performance, and what that means for your investments in 2026.

Over the past year, markets and the economy have been defined by a handful of consistent themes: tepid economic growth, persistent inflation, weakening labor markets, stretched equity valuations, and geopolitical uncertainty. I expect many of those themes to continue to drive the narrative throughout much of the coming year.

The economy and markets may face an inflection point in 2026. Financial markets continue to reflect optimism embedded in elevated equity valuations, yet the underlying economy is sending mixed signals. Economic growth has been uneven, and AI capital investment has been masking weakness elsewhere in the economy.

I believe the U.S. consumer holds the key to where the economy goes this year. Yet, with persistently weak consumer confidence driving sentiment trends to record lows and workers facing the weakest job market in over a decade, it’s difficult to envision a situation where consumption can drive meaningful growth over the next year. But don’t discount the Fed or the temptation of fiscal stimulus in an election year.

Uneven Economic Growth: Consumers Will Be Key in 2026

Economic growth was a mixed bag throughout the first half of 2025, with headline numbers often belying the more nuanced reality buried in the details. In the first quarter of 2025, GDP contracted for the first time in three years, driven by a rare surge in imports, as consumers and businesses tried to get ahead of tariffs.

However, that temporary spike in imports and business investment in Q1 led to a dearth of imports and a drawdown in business inventories in Q2, such that the effects were all but netted out.

Figure 1

The shutdown-delayed initial estimate of GDP growth for Q3 (released at the end of December) surprised significantly to the upside, indicating the economy grew at an annualized rate of 4.3% in the quarter, driven by consumer spending, exports, and government spending.

Figure 2

Assuming these estimates are not revised downward in the coming months, the Q3 GDP report was a testament to the resilience of the U.S. consumer, who is facing otherwise weak growth, persistent inflation, and the worst job market in over a decade. Rightfully so, consumer sentiment is the lowest it has been since the summer of 2022, when inflation was nearly 5%, but real GDP grew at an annualized rate of 7%. Notably, the three- and six-month moving averages of consumer sentiment have never been lower, highlighting the strong downward trend in sentiment since 2024 (see Figure 3).

Figure 3

AI investment has been masking weakness elsewhere in the economy. Putting aside the recent uptick in consumer spending and the noise of tariffs, an outsized proportion of economic growth has been driven by capital investment in artificial intelligence. To provide some perspective, investment in AI-related infrastructure (IT equipment, software, and R&D) accounted for more than 30% of GDP growth in Q2 and more than 14% of GDP growth in Q3. In dollar terms, the U.S. economy grew by $438.5 billion in the first nine months of 2025—$202 billion of that (or 46%) was attributable to AI-related investments. Over the past twelve months, real GDP grew by 2.3%; removing AI-related capital investment, that rate would have been just 1.5% (see Figure 4).

This impressive rate of capital investment and the sheer amount of wealth that has piled into the stocks of AI hyperscalers (even at the current lofty valuations) means that economic and financial fortunes are increasingly reliant on continued enthusiasm around the promise of AI. Determined to deliver on that promise at any cost, firms have now started financing additional investment with debt, leveraging their bets and increasing risk. However, the future benefit of this capital investment—both in terms of financial return for firms and the promise of improved economic productivity—is uncertain. It is unlikely that all (or even a large portion) of these AI investments will pay off sufficiently to meet current expectations.

Figure 4

The economy cannot thrive on AI alone. For a country where consumer spending has historically been the primary driver of economic growth, it has been encouraging to see consumption tick up over the past two quarters. However, if there is going to be more durable economic growth in the year ahead, the consumer needs to return in a more consistent and sustainable way. Short of measurable improvements in labor market conditions, inflation, and consumer sentiment, it’s hard to envision a situation where consumption continues to drive meaningful economic growth over the next year.

Weakest Labor Market in Over a Decade

As was the case throughout most of last year, the labor market continues to signal weakness in the economy, in stark contrast to the AI-fueled optimism driving record highs in equity markets. Unemployment is rising, job growth is anemic, wage growth is stagnant, and layoffs continue to rise as employers wrestle with uncertainty and seek to right-size their workforces in the shadow of the post-pandemic hiring boom.

The headline (U-3) unemployment rate now stands at 4.6%. A broader measure (U-4), which includes discouraged job seekers who have dropped out of the labor force, is at 4.9%. Both measures are up half a point from where they were at the end of 2024. Moreover, the number of job openings in the economy has steadily declined since the beginning of the year, as firms have scaled back hiring and layoffs have increased. Meanwhile, people who have jobs are hanging on to them—the quit rate fell to its lowest level since 2020, suggesting workers lack confidence in their ability to switch jobs in this hiring environment.

Figure 5

Excluding the COVID-19 pandemic, this has been the worst trailing twelve-month period of job growth since 2010. Since the beginning of the year, the U.S. economy has created a mere 610,000 jobs. To put that in perspective, that’s 18,000 fewer jobs than were created in the entire fourth quarter of 2024 and 86,000 fewer jobs than were created in the single month of July 2022.

Figure 6

Relative to the past 15 years, this current pace of job creation is abysmal. Over the past twelve months, the number of jobs in the economy has grown at only 77,000 jobs per month, on average. Between 2020 and 2024, the economy created roughly 119,000 jobs per month, on average—that includes March 2020, when nonfarm payrolls shrank by a staggering 20 million jobs in a single month (no, that’s not a typo). Some economists would argue this is an unfair comparison because the post-pandemic job market was uncharacteristically strong. However, in the years between the financial crisis and the pandemic (2011-2019), the economy created roughly 190,000 jobs per month, on average.

But let’s break it down further. It’s actually pretty straightforward to calculate the breakeven rate of job growth—that is, the number of jobs that need to be created in a given period to keep the unemployment rate constant. For a given unemployment rate, the breakeven rate of job growth is:

Where labor force growth is simply the product of population growth and the labor force participation rate. In a recent publication, the Congressional Budget Office estimates that the civilian noninstitutionalized population age 16 and older—those people that could be included in the labor force—will grow by approximately 2.64 million in 2026 (or about 220,000 per month, give or take). With an average labor force participation rate of about 62.5%, that means the economy needs to add about 131,000 jobs per month to maintain the current 4.6% unemployment rate. That’s a far cry from the 77,000 jobs per month we’ve added on average over the past year.

In fairness, new research out of the Dallas Fed suggests that the recent reversal of immigration flows and cyclical changes in labor force participation has caused the monthly break-even rate of employment growth to decrease dramatically from a peak of approximately 250,000 jobs in 2023 to about 30,000 jobs now. While I agree that changing demographic factors are important, I am skeptical of the conclusion. For one, the CBO report I cited already accounts for those shifting demographic trends. Additionally, even if one accepts the 30,000 per month threshold at face value, that is only the level necessary to maintain a constant unemployment rate—it says nothing about the level of job creation necessary to sustain production, income, and consumption at a level consistent with a robust economy. Any way you slice it, the current labor market is one of the weakest in over a decade.

Equity Markets: Asymmetric Risks, Limited Margin of Safety

Equity markets just wrapped up their third consecutive year of double-digit returns—a run that has been unusually narrow in both leadership and narrative. Since 2023, the market cap-weighted S&P 500 index has returned more than 81% (a compounded annualized return of about 21.5% per year), while the equal-weight S&P 500 has returned just 38%.

Figure 7

The outperformance of the cap-weighted index was, of course, fueled by enthusiasm around artificial intelligence, with gains concentrated in a handful of mega-cap technology companies. The top eight stocks by market cap—all technology firms—now dominate the S&P 500, making up nearly 38% of the benchmark index. That’s more than the weighting of the financial, healthcare, industrial, energy, and utilities sectors combined. As such, well-intentioned, passive ETF and index fund investors just hoping to be in “the market” are probably less diversified than they realize, with an outsized proportion of their wealth allocated to just eight firms, in one country, and one industry.

As I have noted here previously, nearly every valuation measure I follow suggests the broad market is overvalued, with most metrics more than two standard deviations above their long-term averages. For example, the total value of the S&P 500 to U.S. GDP is now more than 2.4 standard deviations above its historical trend (see Figure 8)—well above anything we’ve seen in the past 75 years.

At any sign of softness, retail investors (and some professionals) continue to preach their “buy the dip” mantra as if an immediate rebound is ordained—further proof that this FOMO rally has moved beyond logical valuation into a purely emotional state. Any recollection of sustained market drawdowns has apparently been erased from our societal memory.

Figure 8

Meanwhile, the cyclically adjusted earnings yield—the inverse of the often-cited cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings (CAPE) ratio—is trading below its Depression-era level of 3% for only the third time in history (the other two being the late 1990s tech bubble and the COVID-19 pandemic) (see Figure 9).

Figure 9

Looking at earnings yield rather than a P/E ratio is perhaps a more intuitive way of looking at market valuation. It both illustrates how historically overvalued markets currently are and signals information about investors’ expectations for future earnings growth, macroeconomic conditions, and discount rates. Conveniently, it also allows us to compare equity returns with bonds, CDs, and cash.

At these levels, the market is pricing in above-trend real earnings growth, productivity gains that more than offset future cost pressures, structurally higher profit margins, and a benign macroeconomic and policy environment with durable economic growth, solid labor markets, stable prices, and low interest rates. Given the current macro environment, I believe such expectations are overly optimistic: not impossible, but unlikely.

When the earnings yield falls to historically low levels, markets are pricing in future growth as if it’s a certainty. Earnings yields at these levels tend to be associated with lackluster forward 10-year returns. In fact, over the past 125 years, earnings yields below 3% have been followed by 10-year annualized returns between -5.1% and 2.8% (see Figure 10). When markets are priced to perfection, returns become increasingly dependent on outcomes exceeding already optimistic expectations, such that even modest shortfalls in growth can have an outsized impact on returns.

At current valuations, investing in the broad market offers a limited margin of safety and, therefore, asymmetric risk. Unless valuations continue to expand, the potential return for these overvalued stocks is limited while there is significant downside risk from multiple compressions, even without a recession. Investors who continue to pile into the momentum trade or passive market cap-weighted funds that track the broad market are accepting the likelihood of historically low long-term returns in exchange for elevated valuation risk.

Figure 10

This is not to say that all stocks are overvalued—I continue to find good companies with strong balance sheets and cash flows at attractive multiples that the market has largely ignored. For example, the combined market capitalization of stocks in defensive industries (e.g., consumer staples, utilities, healthcare, energy, etc.) has recently hit dot-com era lows (see Figure 11), and I believe some stocks in those industries offer fair expected returns with a greater margin of safety.

Nor is this meant to imply that a recession or even a bear market is imminent. Just because equity valuations are straining historical norms doesn’t mean overvalued stocks cannot move higher from here—bubbles can continue to defy reason as long as speculative funds continue to flow in.

Figure 11

Nevertheless, current conditions suggest pairing smart, selective offense with defensive hedges. I continue to stay true to a value-oriented investment process, but I am tactically hedging equity exposure and keeping extra cash on the sidelines to cushion downside risks and take advantage of good values in equity markets as they arise.

Liquidity and Leverage Drive Late-Cycle Momentum

The U.S. economy has largely grown into the extraordinary amount of cash that was pumped into the system during the COVID-19 pandemic, which up until now has served as a tailwind for asset prices. M2 money supply as a share of GDP has returned to pre-Covid levels for the first time in six years (see Figure 12). Short of additional liquidity being injected into the system, I would expect a bit of normalization in equities as markets become driven less by liquidity and more by fundamentals.

Figure 12

However, concerned about downside risks to the labor market, the Fed has gradually shifted toward more accommodative monetary policy with three consecutive 25 basis point rate cuts between September and December of last year. While the Fed formally ended its policy of quantitative tightening on December 1st—whereby it sought to reduce market liquidity by allowing maturing securities to roll off its balance sheet without reinvesting the proceeds—by mid-December they were once again forced to inject $40 billion of securities purchases to ease pressure in overnight lending markets.

Recent tremors in short-term rates are worth monitoring, as equity markets tend to get a bit wobbly when short-term funding markets are stressed. As I pointed out in a LinkedIn post last fall, the spread between two important short-term rates, the Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) and the Interest Rate on Reserve Balances (IORB)—which is negative when market liquidity is abundant—has turned positive and has been expanding since the Fed cut rates at the end of September, indicating liquidity shortages. This contributed to a brief pause in the equity rally last fall. (The SOFR is a key short-term rate that reflects the cost of overnight borrowing using U.S. Treasuries as collateral. The IORB is a key short-term rate administered by the Fed, which reflects the rate the Fed pays banks on their reserves held at the central bank. Interested readers can learn more about SOFR here and IORB here).

As highlighted in Figure 13, these temporary liquidity events are fairly common at the end of calendar quarters (grey bars), as financial institutions take advantage of short-term funding markets to window-dress their balance sheets. What’s worth watching are the large and increasingly common spikes in spreads that are happening outside these windows, which point to more structural liquidity strains.

Figure 13

Leverage—the use of borrowed funds, derivatives, or leveraged ETFs to control larger investment positions with less capital—has been another source of momentum and is further evidence that this extended bull market is long in the tooth. Margin debt has increased significantly as a share of the money supply. This is a late-cycle phenomenon, as investors’ appetite for risk increases with their fear of missing out, speculative intensity rises, and investors abandon fundamentals to chase prices higher on sentiment. While this increased leverage serves to further amplify asset prices on the way up, it will unconditionally exacerbate drawdowns when the tide eventually turns, as falling prices trigger margin calls, which trigger forced selling, which drives prices lower in a vicious cycle.

Similarly, the increasing use of derivatives by investors and leveraged ETFs to speculate on inflated asset prices has also served to further drive stock prices higher. For example, when an investor purchases a call option to speculate on a stock, the counterparty to that transaction (typically a market maker) is inherently short the stock. To hedge their short call position, the market maker will buy 100 shares of the stock (1 call option controls 100 shares of the underlying stock). At scale, those stock purchases drive up the price of the underlying shares. When stock prices fall, long call positions are closed out, triggering counterparties to sell the stock, driving stock prices lower, and so on. As is always the case, leverage cuts both ways, serving to enhance both gains and losses.

What does this mean for your money?

The economy is not as strong as top-line GDP growth and equity prices would have you believe. The impressive rate of AI capital investment over the past few years has been masking weaknesses elsewhere in the economy. Excluding the boost from AI-related investment, economic growth has been disappointing. If there is going to be more durable economic growth in the year ahead, the consumer will need to return in a more consistent and sustainable way. However, while consumer spending has ticked up recently, the median U.S. consumer is struggling with affordability and facing the weakest labor market in over a decade. With rising layoffs and unemployment, anemic job growth, stagnant wages, and persistently high inflation, it’s hard to envision a situation where consumption drives meaningful economic growth over the next year.

Given current economic conditions, record equity valuations, and the level of liquidity and leverage currently in the market, it’s also hard to imagine another year of double-digit equity market returns.

However, with the political environment in D.C. and current Fed Chairman Powell’s term set to expire in May, it is virtually certain that his successor will be considerably more dovish. Additional rate cuts could be the catalyst that moves markets higher in the second half of the year. While I currently see no economic reason for equities to have another banner year, as the old market adage goes, don’t fight the Fed. Additionally, as a consequence of the OBBA, a significant portion of consumers are likely to see larger than usual tax refunds in 2026, which may also serve as a de facto fiscal stimulus in the first half of the year.

While market valuations remain stretched, I believe there will be enough support from monetary and fiscal policy this year that investors need to stay long equities in 2026. However, I expect the market rally to broaden out this year and believe investors should diversify beyond the AI momentum trade and rotate into overlooked sectors and regions. For example, I continue to find good value in defensive sectors such as consumer staples, utilities, healthcare, and energy, as well as developed international markets. However, I do not believe investors should be passively investing in market cap-weighted strategies. Investors need to pick their spots—buy good companies with strong balance sheets and cash flows at attractive multiples—and actively manage risk through diversification, active hedges, and keeping cash on the sidelines to cushion downside risks and take advantage of good values as they arise.

data.title: Zions: Strong Investment Setup
data.description:
Zions Bancorporation (ZION) beat top and bottom line estimates for the fourth fiscal quarter on Tuesday due to strong net earnings growth and higher net interest margins. The regional lender benefited from higher net interest income -- which reached almost double-digit year-over-year growth -- and showed a moderate expansion in its loan portfolio amid strong credit demand. In my opinion, the regional bank is primed for more growth in 2026 as the U.S. economy is growing and lower interest rates could potentially further spur demand for new loans. Since shares of Zions Bancorporation are trading at a relatively moderate premium to book value, given the industry group average in the mid-size regional banking market, I see revaluation upside for 2026.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Zions Bancorporation a buy in October 2025 -- Credit Trouble Creates Opportunity -- due to the original lender experiencing panic selling related to distressed residential and commercial mortgage loans. Zions Bancorporation, as well as Western Alliance (WAL) at the time, both suffered a deterioration in investor sentiment after the regional banks disclosed an increase in bad loans, raising concerns about credit quality. Shares of Zions Bancorporation recovered nicely from the October sell-off (as I expected) and have more upside potential as the bank’s loan portfolio and net interest income continue to grow.

ZION Beats Q4’25 Estimates

The banking season has been great so far, with major Wall Street banks such as Bank of America (BAC) and U.S. Bancorp (USB) beating estimates by wide margins, mainly due to strong net interest income tailwinds. Zions Bancorporation also beat earnings estimates for Q4’25 on Tuesday: it reported $1.64 in adjusted EPS, outmatching the consensus estimate of $1.57. The revenue figure came in at $902M, beating the average prediction by $37.4M.

Like most (regional and Wall Street) banks that have reported earnings so far, Zions Bancorporation benefited from a strong interest rate environment that helped the lender grow its net interest income, which in turn was a major catalyst for the bank’s net earnings growth. The regional bank generated net earnings of $262M in Q4'25, showing a massive 31% year-over-year growth rate, due to strong performance in fee-generating business as well as a well-performing core lending business.

Most importantly, Zions Bancorporation also ended the fourth quarter with exceptional credit quality, which underpins my October argument that the bank was not suffering from a deteriorating balance sheet. In fact, the lender's net charge-off ratio -- which measures incremental increases in bad debt write-offs as a percentage of average loans outstanding -- was only 0.05% as of the end of Q4'25, showing a quarter-over-quarter drop-off of 32 basis points.

The bank’s net interest income came in at $683M, showing 9% year-over-year growth due to Zions Bancorporation benefiting from an expansion in its underlying loan portfolio and a robust overall credit environment. Further, the bank benefited from customer deposit migration as well as a reduction in borrowings in the last quarter, which boosted ZIONS' net interest margin to 3.31% (+0.03 PP Q/Q).

The fourth quarter was also the eighth consecutive quarter of net interest margin growth for the regional bank, and the outlook for 2026 is favorable as well, in my opinion. While the Federal Reserve is likely going to push federal fund rates lower in the next twelve months, the U.S. economic backdrop is strong (growing U.S. GDP, moderating inflation), which could serve to ignite consumer and business spending, driving loan originations for banks like Zions Bancorporation.

Lower interest rates in 2025 already lifted demand for new credit, especially in the lender’s commercial segment, which creates upside for Zions Bancorporation’s net earnings growth in 2026. The bank’s average total loans reached $60.8M at the end of the fourth quarter, showing 2.5% growth year-over-year.

Zions Bancorporation's fee-driven income gained 9% quarter-over-quarter to $177M, with the bank seeing especially strong tailwinds in its capital markets business (+54% Q/Q) as well as higher retail and business banking fees (+5% Q/Q). Since I expect a strong equity market performance in 2026, driven by moderating inflation and strong U.S. economic growth, it is reasonable to expect a continuation of this strong trend in non-interest income for Zions Bancorporation in 2026.

Zions' Valuation

The regional lender is growing its book value rapidly amid a strong lending environment (high-interest rates, falling inflation, strong credit demand): its book value per share increased 18% year-over-year to $48.18 in Q4'25. Zions is currently priced at a price-to-book ratio of 1.28X, which makes the bank the cheapest mid-sized banking enterprise in the industry group. The industry group here includes U.S. Bancorp (USB), PNC Financial Services (PNC), and Western Alliance.

Western Alliance, which I also recommended during last year's October sell-off -- Don't Worry About First Brands -- is the second-cheapest regional lender with a P/B ratio of 1.33X. In my opinion, since Zions reported impressive net earnings growth on a dollar basis and faces strong tailwinds in its core lending operations in 2026, the bank could trade at a much higher price-to-book ratio, assuming that Zions Bancorporation's credit quality doesn't deteriorate.

In my opinion, a price-to-book ratio of ~1.4X could be appropriate for Zions Bancorporation, which equals the industry group average, considering that the bank is doing just as well as other regional banks, including U.S. Bancorp, which I just covered as well: A Solid Buy Case. A 1.4X industry group-like P/B ratio implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $67.45/share for Zions, which calculates to upside revaluation potential of 14%.

Risks with Zions

The biggest risk for the regional lender is an accelerating timeline for the Federal Reserve’s federal funds rate cuts in 2026, which poses a risk to the bank’s net earnings growth as well as net interest income specifically. What would get me to change my opinion on Zions Bancorporation is if the regional lender were to see a decelerating pace of net interest income growth in 2026 or if it were to suffer an increase in bad loans (higher net charge-off ratios) that would be indicative of deteriorating balance sheet quality.

Final Thoughts

Zions Bancorporation submitted a strong earnings sheet for the fourth fiscal quarter on Tuesday that was characterized by strong net earnings growth, driven by its fee-generating businesses and higher net interest income in its core lending operation. Further, the bank saw a moderate expansion of its loan portfolio and a drop-off in net charge-offs, highlighting improving asset quality. I like that Zions Bancorporation is growing its book value per share at double digits, has strong asset quality, and is seeing growth in its capital markets business... which will make Zions less dependent on the interest rate environment for its revenue and earnings growth going forward.

data.title: NETSTREIT: A Texas-Based Retail REIT That Could Keep The Bulls Charging
data.description:
Today's Pick: A Texas-Based Retail REIT Whose Share Price Has Grown

Today we are exploring a retail REIT called NETSTREIT Corp (NTST), which is up around +13% since I recommended holding on to it in my May 2025 article, and also up around +2% since my upgrade to a buy in September.

In both cases, my optimism was driven by their portfolio growth.

Since my last coverage, this REIT came out with new earnings results in late October, and also will announce its next ones soon on Feb. 10th, so this article is a preview to get ahead of that.

For readers new to this stock, according to its Seeking Alpha profile this Dallas-based REIT "specializes in acquiring single-tenant net lease retail properties nationwide."

Continue reading to see how I apply my REIT analysis process in action.

Research Methodology.

My methodology in 2026 is focused on risk management and not simply building a diversified portfolio, so this article makes use of a risk worksheet to assess both downside risks and upside opportunities, along with the expected business impact of each category, to determine a weighted rating score.

The Thesis: Reaffirm Prior Buy Rating

My thesis this time reaffirms my prior buy rating, despite considering additional angles and new data since my last coverage. Today's article will show how despite some technical patterns pointing to a neutral view, most all fundamental categories are driving bullishness, with the strongest being resilient macro retail forecasts and population growth, along with the company's own net portfolio growth lately.

Additionally, it impressed on FFO growth, dividend risk, and future upside forecasts.

Continue reading to learn more about how I came to this conclusion, and the evidence supporting it.

Price & Technicals: No Clear Buy Or Sell Signals

Before talking about some fundamental topics, let's consider technical patterns we can observe in the price chart below, which compares the 50-day SMA to the 200-day SMA:

In this example, we see a buy signal (green circle / golden cross pattern) last spring when the 50-day SMA crossed above the 200-day. Lately, the two moving averages shown are parallel but very close together.

This appears to me to indicate a neutral/hold pattern or a watch and wait until either a breakdown occurs to the downside or further upward momentum occurs. There are no clear buy or sell signals right now.

Although this is not the only type of technical chart, and for the sake of brevity I only use this one, I certainly welcome reader comments on your view of this pattern.

Macro Demand: Demographic Factors Come Into Focus, And Consumer Sentiment

Let's consider macro demand drivers outside the company's control but that could impact it.

First, it is worth understanding the specific niche this REIT is in, since I love to say that no two REITs are exactly alike.

We can learn from the company's info deck (pg 1) that this REIT has a diversified retail exposure with top segments that include grocery, dollar stores, and pharmacies, which I think could fall into the consumer essential spend category, but also there are consumer discretionary spend categories there too:

So, I believe the more discretionary spending categories of retail could be impacted by future recession risk, eroding consumer spending confidence.

A major bank like J.P. Morgan had the following 2026 outlook in its late-December note:

Looking at the economy, J.P. Morgan Global Research forecasts a 35% probability of a U.S. and global recession in 2026, and sticky inflation will likely remain a prevailing theme.

So, this forecast ought to add some confidence to retail REIT investors, I think. Even though the REIT itself just owns the properties and does not directly run the stores, it certainly could see elevated risk if suddenly all its retail tenants are seeing a huge drop in customers.

Further, this month Deloitte published a note highlighting data from a survey of retail executives. We see in the chart below that survey respondents have at least 40% expectation that revenues will grow 5 to 9% YoY in 2026, which I think could add to the upside case for retail REITs as well as their tenants:

The other macro factor to consider is geography. From this REIT's infodeck, we see that this REIT has considerable exposure in so-called "sunbelt" states like Texas (its home state), Georgia, and North Carolina:

We can see that Texas, for instance, drives at least +15% of ABR (annualized base rent). So, as someone whose equities research firm happens to be founded and registered in Austin the state capital, the first thing that comes to mind is population growth, since I lived through it firsthand. More people could mean more spending in various stores.

Here is a future population growth forecast for Texas, published this past November by the University of Texas San Antonio, citing research by the Texas Demographic Center:

Here, we can see that even the mid-migration forecast points to the state growing steadily to 42.6MM people by the year 2060. As a future-minded investor and analyst, I think such forecasts can add to the upside case for retail REITs as the demand for stores grows.

So, I continue to see a favorable macro environment for this retail REIT, and think the business impact will be high.

Company Growth: Net Portfolio Expansion and Development Are On The Table

Getting the macroeconomic/demographic trends out of the way, let's consider company-specific growth factors for this REIT.

Since my last coverage of this stock, the last quarterly results published in October could offer some points in this regard.

For instance, from the Q3 investor deck (pg. 15), what you need to know is that this REIT is continually investing in its portfolio growth, with Q3 investments seeing YoY growth compared to the same quarter a year prior:

My opinion is that portfolio growth could drive future revenue /rents from those new properties, and that matters when thinking about the top line. In fact, I've learned that REITs are some of the easiest businesses to understand if you take the time to, since leases are typically longer-term in nature and can give you a sense of rental income stability and growth. This is different from a business selling a one-time product.

REITs also dispose of properties, or "recycle" them to acquire higher-yielding ones. In this case, though, from that same investor deck we see that only 24 properties were disposed of in Q3, so that indicates net portfolio growth.

In addition, an active development pipeline is something I look for, and from the Q3 press release (pg. 2) we see that this REIT has 2 active developments it is funding, so again I think that could help drive future upside via portfolio growth:

To add an additional angle to this story, Seeking Alpha data shows expected forward revenue growth of +19%, much higher than the sector average which is closer to 4%, so I think that should add more confidence to investors focused on the top line.

Of course, I have been talking about risk management lately in articles so the risk to this story is whether development execution goes as planned or faces construction delays and cost overruns, something not uncommon with big projects.

Overall, however, I am highly bullish in this category, supported by the evidence above.

Profitability: Several Metrics Going In The Right Direction for this REIT

Next, let's consider some metrics involving profitability.

The metric of FFO growth is a figure often looked at in REITs, and from Seeking Alpha data we know that this REIT saw 3 year FFO CAGR of +13.1%, much higher than the sector average, and in the chart below over a period of 10 years shown there seems to be a steady upward growth trend in FFO:

I think this adds to the bullish case for investors looking for a REIT with an already proven model of FFO growth.

Another metric, the EBITDA margin, relates to core earnings efficiency (excluding property depreciation) and so here is how Netstreit compares to a Texas-based peers, Whitestone REIT (WSR), which I picked due to its exposure to Sunbelt markets:

The data shows Netstreit with an EBITDA margin of +79%, far higher than this peer in a similar niche, so it adds to my confidence in this REIT's ability to drive earnings efficiency.

In this article, I will also add another angle and this time comparing the operating margin vs several large retail REITS or REITs with a large retail exposure nationally:

In this comparison, with an operating margin of 36%, Netstreit is on the lower end of this peer group but its trend line is also showing improvemennt since 2023, so that is a positive sign to mention.

In this case, I think the positives outweigh any headwinds so I am bullish about profitability. Consider also that the analyst consensus implies 4 - 7% YoY FFO growth for FY26 and FY27, which should help further support my conviction.

Competitive Position: Reliant on Several Major Retail Brands, Not Just One

When it comes to competitive position, here is my view on this REIT.

First of all, it has tenant relationships with some very large, well-known brands covering retail, pharmacy, supermarkets, and home improvement, with the majority of its top 10 tenants being considered "investment grade." Consider the infographic from their October investor deck (pg 5), which came out after my last article on this stock:

This is important, I think, because it is these brands that the end-consumer sees and shops with, and is loyal to, while the REIT/landlord is like behind the scenes.

Further, from available market data on approximate market cap, I made this table comparing market caps among major REITs focusing on retail:

We can see that Netstreit, with a market cap around $1.5B, is on the lower middle end of this peer group, with its two Texas peers actually smaller by market cap.

This indicates that Netstreit is increasingly becoming a bigger player in this niche, and if that trend continues it could drive future upside.

While its competitive position is partly tied to relationships with major retailers, from a risk view we also see in the prior graphic that its top tenant (Dollar General) drives no more than 5.4% of ABR, so there is no tenant over-concentration.

Given these points, I am bullish when it comes to market positioning.

Balance Sheet Risk: A Modest D/E, with Investment-Grade From Fitch

We cannot ignore the topic of balance sheet risk, especially since I've been writing about the importance of risk management.

For one thing, let's consider a comparison of debt-to-equity vs peers:

With a D/E of 0.86, which has risen lately it seems, Netstreit lies somewhere in the middle of this peer group, and is not a major outlier for leverage risk.

I think this could appeal to more debt-averse investors and analysts.

Further, to consider what a major rating agency thinks about this REIT, the company reported in December that it got a BBB- grade from rating agency Fitch, which is essentially just barely investment-grade.

The press release said:

The BBB- issuer rating reflects the Company’s solid property portfolio and stable operating performance as demonstrated by resilient pandemic-era performance, with occupancy over 99%.

I would point out an important point there, and that is pandemic-era resilience. This shows that this REIT was able to survive and overcome a major market shock that hit retailers. It adds to the likelihood that this REIT could be resilient if future such market shocks were to occur, though not a guarantee of course.

Given the evidence in this section, my bullish sentiment continues to be supported here.

Dividend Risk: A Proven Grower, With an Acceptable Payout Ratio

When I started writing about and investing in REITs, I was more interested in building a high-yield portfolio, but since then I am moved towards having a greater focus on dividend risk.

In the case of this REIT, income statement data shows in the most recent quarterly result it had an AFFO per share of $0.33, while dividend data shows a quarterly dividend of $0.22. This implies a payout ratio around 67%. My benchmark has been 75% or less, so it is within my risk tolerance. Some readers, however, may have a lower threshold and I welcome a discussion on that in the comments.

The importance of a lower payout ratio is related to having enough of a cash cushion after dividends get paid out.

Another dividend risk factor is questioning whether this REIT has been able to steadily grow its dividend over time.

If we take a 5-year period, which is adequate and long enough, the answer is yes. The REIT saw a 5-year dividend CAGR of +23%, far better than the sector average.

We can also visualize it in the chart below:

It indicates there were no major dividend cuts in this period which saw the tail-end of the global pandemic as well as a period of high inflation and then high interest rates in recent years.

Again, here too I am bullish and the data supports such a sentiment.

Valuation vs Upside: The Numbers Point To Further Upside, While Valued Reasonably Now

In this final section, I made a worksheet using valuation data, earnings consensus data, and price targets:

In this worksheet, we see the fwd P/FFO is somewhat elevated to the sector average, perhaps driven by positive FFO consensus and historically positive FFO growth. The price to book multiple is considerably below the sector average, indicating a possible undervaluation opportunity. The market is willing to pay a 1.19x multiple on book value.

At the same time, both my price target and that of the avg. Wall St consensus indicates between 6 - 11% further upside potential. So, not one forecast but two forecasts pointing to further upside. This is significant.

For this reason, I am calling it a modest valuation opportunity with future upside potential, and therefore justification to be bullish.

Challenge To My Thesis: Interest Rate Risk Impacting REITs

A factor that could challenge my bullish view of this stock could come from a common theme I have mentioned impacting REITs in general, and that is interest rate risk.

NetSTREIT remains exposed to rising interest rates now and in the future, if we are to consider longer-term REIT investing and not day trading, which this article is not really aimed for.

Higher rates on commercial real estate or construction loans increase borrowing costs and can make new acquisitions and developments more expensive, slowing growth and driving up the cost of capital. They also can put pressure on REIT valuations.

Related to this in a way is higher construction costs in the future, or cost inflation, as that could drive up the need to borrow more and higher cost.

What could mitigate this, of course, is if this REIT continues to show strong positive cashflow from existing properties. I think that is a key factor that many eyes will focus on in the upcoming earnings results.

Conclusion: A Retail REIT Worth Owning

To wrap up today's article, my conclusion is that this REIT is worth owning and in the very least worth additional research and due diligence by investors.

I've shown how both macro factors and company-specific portfolio factors are in its favor, as is its proven ability to generate FFO growth while also achieving investment-grade coverage from Fitch, a major milestone for a retail REIT with a much smaller market cap than some peers in this space.

Ultimately, the long-term play here is betting that American consumers, particularly those in the Sunbelt region, will keep shopping at those major-brand retailers this REIT leases to, particularly in the necessary-spending categories like groceries and pharmacy needs.

data.title: The Next Big Theme: January 2026
data.description:
Data Centers

Momentum Builds as AI Reshapes Digital Infrastructure

We believe the global data center market is poised for strong, sustained growth as artificial intelligence drives demand for compute infrastructure. Estimates suggest 14% annual growth through 2030, potentially adding 100 gigawatts of capacity and effectively doubling worldwide data center power load. AI workloads already account for around 25% of all data center computing, and with inference tasks—the real-time delivery of AI results—overtaking training workloads, firms are prioritizing geographically distributed facilities to optimize performance.1 Behind this surge is a broad range of participants, including cloud hyperscalers, major tech companies, semiconductor and memory vendors, energy providers, and real estate developers securing land for future builds. Power availability and energy infrastructure are critical site determinants, pushing data center developers to invest not just in buildings and chips, but also in the energy capacity needed to keep them running. The result is a robust multi-sector expansion underpinned by AI’s insatiable demand for compute, capacity, and connectivity.

Defense Technology

U.S. Army Pushes Next-Gen Autonomous Drone Capabilities

The U.S. Army is advancing a major effort to develop a large short/vertical takeoff and landing (S/VTOL) unmanned aerial system (UAS) aimed at fielding an advanced Group 4-class drone by 2028. Unlike legacy runway-dependent systems, this next-generation platform is designed to operate without runways, incorporate autonomous mission execution, and continue missions even if communications are jammed—lessons drawn from recent conflicts like Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.2 For the Defense Tech theme, this project reflects growing momentum for autonomous, modular, and resilient unmanned systems designed for contested environments. It highlights how the rising demand for—and investment in—advanced sensors, mission software, cybersecurity, and scalable architectures is fostering innovation from traditional primes and startups alike.

Artificial Intelligence

Competition for AI Capital Accelerates

Leading AI company OpenAI is in talks with e-commerce and cloud giant Amazon about a potential investment that could exceed $10 billion, a move that would deepen ties between the two firms. The potential deal would reportedly link the investment to OpenAI’s use of Amazon’s custom AI chips and cloud infrastructure, strengthening Amazon Web Services’ position as a major supplier in the competitive AI compute market. More broadly, an investment of this scale—which could boost OpenAI’s valuation well above $500 billion—underscores that AI leadership depends not only on algorithms, but also on access to massive capital, scalable cloud infrastructure, and reliable computing resources.3

FinTech

Coinbase’s Expansion Signals the Rise of “Everything Exchanges”

Coinbase has expanded its platform to include commission-free U.S.-listed stock trading in its brokerage accounts and prediction markets for U.S. users, marking a major shift from a crypto-only exchange to a broader multi-asset financial hub. U.S. users can now trade stocks and ETFs directly within the Coinbase app alongside digital assets and participate in Kalshi-powered prediction markets tied to real-world events such as elections and macroeconomic data. This rollout is part of Coinbase’s everything exchange strategy, which also includes futures, perpetuals, and plans for tokenized equities and stablecoin-powered services.4 For the FinTech theme, the strategy blurs traditional lines between crypto exchanges and mainstream financial platforms, intensifying competition with brokerage apps like Robinhood. It also underscores growing investor demand for unified, multi-asset platforms and highlights the growth of stablecoins, tokenization, and on-chain markets in mainstream finance. As digital asset firms expand into traditional finance functions, regulatory clarity and infrastructure adoption are likely to be key determinants of success.

AI Semiconductors

TSMC’s 2nm Breakthrough Points to a New Wave of Chip Innovation

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC)(TSM) has begun mass production of its cutting-edge 2-nanometer (nm) semiconductor chips. The company describes these next-gen chips, built by using an advanced nanosheet transistor structure, as the most advanced technology in the industry in terms of density and energy efficiency. Production is underway at TSMC’s major fabrication facilities in Hsinchu and Kaohsiung, Taiwan as global demand for high-performance, energy-efficient computing continues to surge, particularly for AI, data centers, and mobile devices. TSMC counts major technology clients, including Nvidia and Apple, among its users.5 The ramp-up of 2 nm chips highlights the rapid pace of innovation at the leading edge of chip technology, with broad implications for AI computing, consumer electronics, and global tech supply chains.

CleanTech

Corporate Procurement Emerges as a Clean Energy Catalyst

Corporate demand for clean firm energy—carbon-free power sources that can deliver reliable electricity on demand like new nuclear, fusion, geothermal, and hydropower—is rapidly gaining traction across the United States. Corporate buyers have announced over 6 gigawatts of new clean firm energy capacity across 10 states, with more than half of that total unveiled in 2025 alone, signaling rapid adoption beyond pilot projects. More broadly, in the first three quarters of 2025, companies announced 20.4 gigawatts of new clean energy capacity of all types, underscoring the scale of private-sector demand shaping the energy transition.6 These deals highlight how corporations may be moving beyond intermittent renewables to secure dependable, round-the-clock clean power, while helping to de-risk and commercialize next-generation technologies. Together, they point to a maturing clean energy market and long-term opportunities in decarbonization.

data.title: South Korean GDP Shrank In Fourth Quarter As Fiscal Stimulus Fades
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Downside surprise came from the contraction of exports and investment

We previously projected fourth-quarter South Korean GDP growth to slow to 0.4% quarter-on-quarter, seasonally adjusted, as fiscal stimulus waned even as strong chip exports likely supported overall export growth. Today's data showed that the economy contracted instead. The slowdown in consumption growth was in line with our expectations. But declines in investment and exports diverged from our estimates. Weak GDP exports likely indicate that robust chip exports are driven by higher prices, while auto and machinery exports in real terms dropped due to tariffs and weak global demand. For investment, transportation equipment investment declined sharply, partially offset by IT investment growth.

GDP growth by expenditure

GDP is expected to rebound in 1Q26

Although the 4Q25 growth was weaker than expected, we are keeping our 2026 GDP growth unchanged at 2.0% year on year. Stronger-than-expected chip demand is likely to boost exports and facility investment. After five years of contraction, the construction sector is expected to enter a cyclical recovery. Forward-looking construction orders data have rebounded since last year, and much of the project-finance restructuring has been completed. Private consumption should increase moderately. While a significant rise in domestic equities, expanded welfare programmes, and improved consumer sentiment are likely to be supportive factors, stringent housing regulations and a weak Korean won may temper overall optimism.

GDP is expected to grow 2.0% YoY in 2026 compared to 1.0% in 2025

BoK watch

The weaker-than-expected 4Q25 GDP outcome is not likely to affect the Bank of Korea’s near-term rate trajectory. Most temporary policy influences are subsiding, and the underlying growth trend is showing signs of recovery. We expect the BoK to revise its 2026 GDP forecast from 1.8% YoY to 2.0% when it updates its projections in February. The recovery is likely to be uneven, and the BoK is still concerned about financial instability. As such, the BoK will keep its current neutral tone for an extended period.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The summer’s short squeeze, which boosted low-quality stocks, continued in the fourth quarter. The UBS High Short Interest Index rose more than 25% during the first two weeks of the quarter while major stock market averages barely budged. During this period, talk of a bubble grew louder and eroded the confidence of investors who had bet on high-flying shares of unprofitable companies with unproven business models. As their collective anxiety intensified, the speculative fever that had gripped the market for much of the previous six months began to break.

In the ensuing weeks, a sense of market normalcy slowly emerged. Many investors started questioning narratives that had fueled the mania, shifting their focus from making a quick buck or keeping pace with benchmarks to considering business durability, financial strength, and profitability. The promise of artificial intelligence (AI) remained, as did expectations for significant investment in AI infrastructure. But investors began to scrutinize the funding for those investments and the future revenue and earnings available to companies in the AI ecosystem. Their gaze also may have broadened a bit, allowing them to see a larger set of attractive industries and companies that had been obscured by their excitement about AI.

Value stocks benefited from this intra-quarter market rotation. After lagging in October, they improved in November and December to end the quarter ahead of growth stocks in all market-cap segments. Total return for the Russell Midcap Value® Index, the Carillon Chartwell Mid Cap Value Fund’s benchmark, was 1.4% in the fourth quarter. The best-performing sectors in the index were healthcare, information technology, and communication services. The worst-performing sectors were real estate, consumer discretionary, and utilities.

Fund Review

Top securities

Globus Medical (GMED) sells implantable devices and surgical instruments used in a range of spinal and orthopedic procedures. The company reported stronger growth in sales of its core spine products and margin expansion from synergies, raising guidance to signal confidence in its sustained momentum.

Dollar Tree (DLTR) is a discount retailer that provides low-priced discretionary items and consumables. The company reported accelerating same-store sales growth supported by its multi-price point strategy.

Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX) is one of North America’s largest trucking and logistics companies. Shares were boosted by optimism that trucking supply is normalizing and by expectations for regulatory changes that could tighten capacity and support future pricing.

Kirby (KEX) transports liquid cargo on inland and coastal waterways and provides engine services, power systems, and oilfield equipment. The company benefitted from improving marine utilization and pricing as well as robust growth in power generation services.

Cognizant Technology Solutions (CTSH), a global information technology services and consulting firm, was rewarded after reporting accelerating revenue growth, expanding margins, and strong large-deal momentum.

Bottom securities

FMC manufactures agricultural chemicals, including insecticides, herbicides, and fungicides. The company’s share price was pressured by concerns about accelerating loss of market share and mounting pricing pressure from generic competitors in its core diamide portfolio.

Outlook

Low-quality leadership in 2025, especially during the short squeeze of late summer and early fall, was a significant challenge that negatively impacted the Fund’s relative performance. Historically, such headwinds have dissipated after six to nine months. This latest episode began in April, so the timing of the fourth-quarter rotation is encouraging; we’re optimistic that market conditions are poised to continue normalizing.

We remained committed to our philosophy and process throughout the past year, continuing to make investment decisions that prioritized value, quality, and the potential for fundamental improvement. We were also more active than usual, aiming to take advantage of macroeconomic-driven volatility and the extreme performance bifurcation in the mid-cap universe driven by narrow market leadership.

We believe these opportunistic actions enhanced the fund's portfolio and that many of its holdings, particularly the newest additions, appear to be attractively valued. Our team will continue diligently pursuing promising investment opportunities for the Fund while working prudently to maintain a well-balanced portfolio. The Fund’s current positioning reflects this bottom-up process with modest overweights in the industrials, healthcare, and materials sectors and modest underweights in the financials, communication services, and information technology sectors.

Definitions

Artificial intelligence (AI) – Technology that enables computers to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Destocking – The reduction in the inventory that a company holds, either through market demand or company decisions to reduce or hold less of a particular product or products.

Growth investing – a stock-buying strategy that focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance – Statements from managers of publicly traded companies about their expectations for near-term profits or losses.

Headwind – An event or market force that hinders the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

Market capitalization, or market cap – The total dollar market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

Narrow – When a few data outliers result in a mean that is substantially larger (or smaller) than the median of an index, then its performance is being driven by a “narrow” selection of companies.

Original equipment manufacturers (OEM) – Make parts for use in other firms’ finished products.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Positioning – Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Quality investing – Investment strategies that seek to invest in companies with low debt, stable earnings, consistent asset growth, and strong corporate governance, as reflected in metrics such return to equity and debt-to-equity ratios, as well as to earnings variability.

Relative performance – Performance calculated with similar and comparable assets to determine the value of the assets relative to each other.

Rotation – the movement of investments in securities from one industry, sector, factor, or asset class to another as market participants react to or try to anticipate the next stage of the economic cycle.

Short squeeze – When the price of an asset rises sharply, forcing short sellers to buy it to avoid incurring excessive losses. Those purchases put more upward pressure on the asset price.

Total return – The actual rate of return of an investment or investments over a given period of time. Total return includes interest, capital gains, dividends, and distributions realized over the specified period.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Benchmark Index

The Russell Midcap® Value Index, the Fund’s benchmark index, measures the performance of those Russell Midcap companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

data.title: XPO Logistics: A High-Quality Business That Is Fully Priced
data.description:
In September 2025, I initiated coverage for XPO Logistics (XPO) with a hold rating. As one of the largest providers of less-than-truckload freight transportation services, the company stood out with a disciplined approach towards capacity expansion, favoring unit revenue strength in a challenging freight market. However, the upside I saw was limited to 8%. The stock, however, has returned 15.5% since the publication of my first report, and for that reason I am revisiting the name with an updated price assessment.

XPO Logistics Navigates A Tough Market Environment

The freight and logistics market is a huge market. According to Mordor Intelligence, the total addressable market was $6.4 trillion in 2025 with the market expected to grow 5% annually to $8.1 trillion by 2030. Seemingly, that offers an attractive, stable growth opportunity. However, the size of the market also attracts many players that want a piece of the pie. The result is that the market is highly fragmented and extremely competitive, which can lead to supply-demand imbalances amplified by temporary macroeconomic weaknesses.

For many freight and logistics companies, the challenges started after travel restrictions eased during the pandemic period. It led to consumers changing spending habits and more freight capacity coming online. Freight and logistics companies had taken all opportunities to grow capacity during the lockdowns as supply was limited and demand was high. The end of lockdowns reversed that. Shortly after, the market was also hit by inflation and high-energy prices, putting pressure on the cost of services and demand once again. The trade war did not help either, and as a result, the freight and logistics market is going through a multi-year reset on the supply-demand mechanism.

Opportunities to cut costs include capacity cuts, thereby improving unit revenues, but other measures include in-sourcing of third-party operations. Cash can be preserved through a reduction in capital expenditures. XPO has the advantage that it can capitalize on a strong reputation.

Wall Street Expects Stable Revenues And Lower Earnings

That the market remains in a challenging position can be seen in the expectations for the fourth quarter. Revenue is expected to be between $1.91-$2.01 billion, with a consensus of $1.95 billion marking 1.7% growth. Over the past six months, analysts have revised revenue expectations downward by 1% with six analysts revising estimates up and eight analysts revising estimates down.

On earnings per share, we see that analysts expect $0.71-$0.82 with a consensus EPS estimate of $0.77, marking a 14% decline with all analysts revising estimates downward over the past three months. So, we see that while revenues are expected to hold rather well, cost remains an issue, and that provides somewhat of an indication that there still is so much capacity in the market that passing on higher costs or higher unit costs to customers is not possible.

XPO Stock Is Fairly Valued At Current Prices

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

XPO Logistics has exceeded my price target. However, at current prices, we see no strong upside for the stock. The stock is trading 5% above the implied price target, which I believe represents a fair valuation. EBITDA estimates have declined by 0.8%, but free cash flow estimates have increased by 5.5% driven by stronger cash conversion, which I believe reflects working capital management and cash collections. So, while the stock price did increase, at current price levels we see no upside but also no substantial downside. As a result, I am reiterating my hold rating.

Margins are expected to be flat for 2025 and tick up in the years after to around 17.5%. One of the drivers of the margins is an outflow of drivers expected in the US driven by stricter language requirements. This may tighten supply and drive up unit prices. Free cash flow is expected to continue growing as capital expenditures have largely been tapered. The company has a $750 million repurchase program, which I believe will ramp up in the years ahead. While the company returns value to shareholders, we will see the cash balance increase and net debt coming down. My current expectation is that the company will move its net-debt leverage from the upper limit of 3x to the lower limit of 2x. Anything between 2x and 3x is what I consider healthy.

Conclusion: The Entry Point Is Lacking For XPO

If you are happy with the entry point around fair value, then the current stock prices do provide that, as the stock is just 5% overvalued. However, I believe that provides little margin of safety, and that means that XPO currently does not offer an attractive entry point. The company does trade at a premium to peers and I believe its scale and premium that the company can charge due to its service reputation justify that. However, we note that all of that is already fully priced in. So, the premium service, premium EV/EBITDA, and strong balance sheet are already fully valued and provide no support for a buy rating.
